nodes.text: Introductory physics links motion, force, energy, momentum, fields, heat, and waves into one coherent system of laws.
Kinematics describes motion using displacement, velocity, acceleration, and time, without first asking what causes the motion.
Velocity is the rate of change of position: v = dx/dt.
Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity: a = dv/dt = d^2x/dt^2.
For constant acceleration, v = u + at.
For constant acceleration, s = ut + (1/2)at^2.
These constant-acceleration equations work only when acceleration remains constant over the time interval being studied.
Dynamics explains why motion changes by connecting forces to acceleration and interactions.
Newton's first law: if net force is zero, an object remains at rest or moves with constant velocity.
Newton's second law: the net force on an object equals mass times acceleration, ΣF = ma.
Newton's third law: if object A exerts a force on object B, then B exerts an equal and opposite force on A.
Near Earth's surface, weight is the gravitational force W = mg.
Friction opposes relative motion or impending motion between surfaces and often converts mechanical energy into thermal energy.
An object must have a net force acting on it in order to keep moving at constant velocity.
Energy methods track how systems store and transfer the ability to do work.
Work is the transfer of energy by a force acting through a displacement: W = F s cos(θ) for a constant force.
Kinetic energy is KE = (1/2)mv^2.
Near Earth's surface, gravitational potential energy is U = mgh relative to a chosen reference level.
The work-energy theorem states that net work equals change in kinetic energy: W_net = ΔKE.
If only conservative forces act, total mechanical energy remains constant: KE_i + U_i = KE_f + U_f.
Potential energy depends on the chosen zero level, but changes in potential energy determine physical predictions.
Momentum methods are especially powerful for collisions, explosions, and short interaction times.
Linear momentum is p = mv.
Impulse changes momentum: J = FΔt = Δp for constant force over a short interval.
In an isolated system, total momentum is conserved: Σp_before = Σp_after.
Momentum is conserved in all isolated collisions, while kinetic energy is conserved only in elastic collisions.
Kinetic energy is conserved in every collision.
Electromagnetism explains electric forces, fields, potential, current flow, and magnetic effects.
Coulomb's law gives the electric force magnitude between point charges: F = k|q1q2|/r^2.
Electric field is force per unit charge: E = F/q.
Potential difference is work per unit charge: V = W/q.
Ohm's law for an ohmic conductor is V = IR.
Electrical power can be written as P = IV = I^2R = V^2/R.
A changing magnetic flux can induce an emf, which is the basis of electromagnetic induction.
A battery always provides the same current no matter what resistance is connected.
Thermodynamics studies heat, temperature, internal energy, work, and entropy.
The first law of thermodynamics is ΔU = Q - W, where Q is heat added to the system and W is work done by the system.
Temperature is related to average microscopic kinetic behavior, whereas heat is energy transferred because of a temperature difference.
In an isolated system, entropy tends to increase until equilibrium is reached.
For an ideal gas, PV = nRT.
Heat and temperature are the same thing.
Wave physics explains oscillations, propagation, interference, and the behavior of light and sound.
Wave speed obeys v = fλ.
When waves overlap, the resulting displacement is the sum of the individual displacements.
Sound waves require a material medium, but electromagnetic waves can travel through vacuum.
Refraction occurs because wave speed changes when a wave enters a new medium.
Sound can travel through a vacuum.
Mechanical energy and thermodynamics are linked because non-conservative work often appears as thermal energy.
Light is an electromagnetic wave, linking optics directly to electric and magnetic theory.
Force connects to momentum through time: since F = dp/dt, a force sustained over time produces impulse.
The net force is the vector sum of all individual forces acting on an object.
The impulse-momentum theorem states that the net impulse on an object equals its change in momentum.
Internal energy is a state function, so its change depends only on the initial and final thermodynamic states, not on the path between them.
The second law of thermodynamics gives a preferred direction to spontaneous processes by requiring total entropy not to decrease in an isolated system.
When a wave enters a new medium, its frequency stays fixed by the source while wavelength changes with speed.
When light refracts into a new medium, its frequency changes because the speed changes.
Acceleration is the bridge between kinematics and dynamics: kinematics describes it from motion, while dynamics explains it through net force.
Force, work, and impulse are complementary views of interaction: the same force can change motion through acceleration, transfer energy through work, and change momentum through impulse.
Thermodynamics links microscopic particle motion to macroscopic variables like temperature, pressure, and internal energy.
Electromagnetic waves unify electric and magnetic ideas by showing that changing fields can propagate through space as light.
Electrical energy transfer in resistive circuits often appears macroscopically as thermal energy, linking circuit power to thermodynamic heating.
Kinematics summarizes motion through position, velocity, acceleration, and the constant-acceleration equations that connect them.
Dynamics explains motion change by combining net force, Newton's laws, weight, and friction into one force-based framework.
Energy methods track work, kinetic energy, potential energy, and conservation principles to predict motion without following every force in detail.
Momentum methods unify impulse, momentum conservation, and collision analysis, especially when forces act briefly or internally.
Electromagnetism combines electric force, field, potential, circuit relations, and induction into one theory of charge and current.
Thermodynamics and wave theory both encode directionality and propagation through structural laws: entropy guides spontaneous change, while wave relations govern transmission and refraction.

metadata.note: Contrasting sound with electromagnetic waves helps clarify why light can propagate in vacuum.

metadata.title: Physics theory graph (gold, theory-first revision)
metadata.source_note: Built from the uploaded introductory physics graph and extended for theory-first controller supervision with sharper law/principle structure, misconception resolution, bridge nodes, and component-summary nodes.
notes: This gold revision emphasizes laws, principles, and cross-theory structure more than concrete applications.
Several new nodes make implicit theory links explicit, especially net force, impulse-momentum, internal energy, the second law, and refraction-frequency reasoning.
Bridge nodes were added to support theory transfer across kinematics, dynamics, energy, momentum, electromagnetism, thermodynamics, and waves.
Summary nodes are included to supervise summarize_cluster compression once local subgraphs become mature.
